topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Dr. Albright was departing. The subject line was sterile, professional—a notification of transition. I stared at the screen, the words swimming before my eyes, refusing to anchor themselves. My hands started to tremble, and I had to grip the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. The familiar hum of the library seemed amplified, every rustle of paper a sharp, unwelcome intrusion. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. I walked to the window, watching the campus quad, but the green seemed muted, the colors washed out, as if the world itself had suddenly lost its saturation. A strange, hollow ache settled in my chest, heavy and cold. I retreated to my room, pacing the worn rug, unable to settle into any single thought.

[story 2]

"So, he's gone," I managed to say, the words sounding thin and brittle even to my own ears. Professor Davies had been my compass for the last two years, the one person who understood the labyrinthine demands of my historical research. Now, the certainty of his absence hung in the air, thick and suffocating. I kept running my fingers over the worn cover of my annotated manuscript, the paper feeling flimsy, inadequate. My focus fractured; I tried to reread the introduction, but the sentences blurred into meaningless shapes. I found myself chewing on the inside of my cheek, a nervous habit I hadn't realized I’d adopted. Every scheduled meeting felt like a tightrope walk over a chasm. I kept checking my phone, expecting a message, a sign, anything to reorient me to the structure I relied on.

[story 3]

The departmental announcement echoed in the hallway, a sudden, sharp sound that seemed to vibrate inside my skull. I stood frozen by the water cooler, the condensation dripping onto my sleeve, but I couldn't feel the coolness. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. I looked down at my shoes, tracing the laces with a restless toe. The routine of my days—the predictable schedule of seminars, the comforting weight of Dr. Albright’s guidance—had just been ripped away. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the fluorescent lights above flicker momentarily. I wanted to speak, to ask a question, but my throat clamped shut. I just stood there, a statue carved from sudden, unformed anxiety, waiting for the ground beneath my feet to shift entirely.
